replies.raw_content: I've never broken an arm. But, I've read about it, I've experienced arm pain, I've broken a toe. There's likely some things I'll never know without having it happen, but we don't divide the world into thos who have broken arms and those who'll never experience the mystery.

nested_replies.raw_content: As someone who's had a period, I can say with certainty that if you've experienced muscle cramps and diarreah you can pretty much imagine what having a period is like. Some goop leaks out of you, sometimes there's discomfort. You don't go to another plane of existence.

Humans have this amazing ability to communicate their experience and to imagine what it's like for things to be different than they are, and transphobes want to throw that in the trash, on the way undermining the very proposition that men and women are equally human beings.
Sorry about the typos, I've been trying to improve in that area, but I'm a bit under the weather... so today you get what you get.

replies.raw_content: @e_urq On the flip side, I was encouraged to see men (outside my family) as mysterious and threatening, not people I could relate to or trust. Understanding my gender has required me to deliberately challenge my impulse to write off guyhood as something I could never truly comprehend

nested_replies.raw_content: @masc_crone Yeah, definitely. There's an impulse for the sexes to see each other as unknowable and it just divides people unnecessarily while propping up inequality.

replies.raw_content: @e_urq My concern with this, specifically in the cis context, is that privileged perspectives *are* real--part of privilege on any axis does involve treating another group's experience as less real and less human because you don't have access to it and society is set up around yours.

nested_replies.raw_content: @e_urq There *is* a significant difference between imagining something and actually living it--like, I think I'm relatively good at visualizing what transmisogyny is like, but there's an abyss between that and experiencing it directly. Losing sight of that abyss strikes me as dangerous.

@lysistrata327 I'd say a couple things to that. First, I'm talking here about the sensations of a human body. We all have access to one and only one. So, none of us can truly know what it's like to be inside ANY body other than our own.

@lysistrata327 When people imbue something magic to inhabiting a body of a certain sex, it's not based on anything as far as I can tell. It's as true to say woman A can never know what it feels like to be woman B as it is to say woman A can never know what it feels like to be man C.

@lysistrata327 What you're talking about is something more like "an experience of discrimination" or "an experience of being part of another culture". And, I'd argue it's very important to have people try to share what those experiences are like the best they can, to activate empathy.
@lysistrata327 I think you're worried about a step beyond that, where someone reads a really good novel about a culture they're not part of and thinks they can speak for that culture.

@lysistrata327 I agree that's really bad, but I think you have to be careful, here, bc it's even worse to act as if people within that culture are unreachable alien beings to the point where no one outside it could have any understanding of those within at all.

Bc that's xenophobia.

@e_urq Yeah, I agree that trying to share them is essential to empathy, but I also think that recognizing that there are experiences with social dimensions that you don't have direct access to is *also* necessary to empathy. Otherwise I think people just end up projecting.

@e_urq I am... er, critical... of a lot of what goes on in GC spaces--there's definitely mystification at play when you start using this stuff to sharply delineate categories, but I'm nervous of running in the opposite direction and erasing things that are real. If that makes sense?

replies.raw_content: @e_urq Yeah. On one hand, I agree that it's probably very difficult to imagine what it must feel like to live as someone who's oppressed in a way you aren't! But amongst other things, this is what literature does: it creates a space in which you can try to imagine it anyway.

nested_replies.raw_content: @e_urq The notiom that trying to understand the traumatic experiences of others is appropriative/harmful is limiting & dangerous. Empathy is part of what makes human society possible, imo. We shouldn't tell people w/privilege they can never understand, we should encourage them to try!
